refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events, maintenance records) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Time. Compare against the 0.97 threshold. Conduct root-cause analysis on any unplanned downtime events to identify systemic failure modes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate and recover from transient faults without requiring manual intervention where safety permits.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that ingests telemetry from all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), calculates real-time availability metrics, detects anomalies, and triggers predictive maintenance alerts.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across individual subsystems (Vehicle self-monitors, Teleoperation self-monitors, Safety Services self-monitors) with periodic aggregation and reporting to a central dashboard.
DP-C: Implement a redundant architecture with hot-standby subsystems and automatic failover to mask transient faults and maintain continuous availability.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (centralized monitoring) with DP-C (redundancy) to achieve both observability and fault tolerance.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a dual-layer availability assurance strategy: (1) Centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that ingests telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems, calculates real-time availability metrics, detects anomalies, and triggers predictive maintenance alerts; (2) Redundant architecture with hot-standby subsystems and automatic failover mechanisms to mask transient faults and maintain continuous operation where safety permits.

potential_sources: DP-D couples availability assurance to telemetry collection (Telemetry interface), tasking continuity (Tasking interface), and safety decision-making (SafetyAlert interface). These couplings are necessary and well-defined.
DP-D introduces a dependency on network reliability (Network Link interface) for centralized monitoring; this is a known risk but acceptable given the high criticality of availability.
DP-D does not create circular dependencies or conflicting design decisions at this level.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a coupled design (multiple subsystems must coordinate), but the coupling is intentional and traceable to functional requirements. The design is not over-constrained.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services is available with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable real-time anomaly detection.
Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be provisioned and maintained within cost and weight constraints.
Risk: Network Link failures could prevent centralized monitoring from detecting distributed failures; mitigation: implement local fault detection and autonomous failover logic in each subsystem.
Risk: Automatic failover could mask safety-critical failures; mitigation: ensure failover logic is safety-aware and coordinated with Safety Services.

Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may have false-positive rates that drive unnecessary maintenance; mitigation: validate algorithms against historical failure data and tune thresholds iteratively.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly address: (1) the role of Safety Services in gating failover decisions, (2) the latency and reliability requirements for the Telemetry and Network Link interfaces, and (3) the criteria for distinguishing safety-driven unavailability from failure-driven unavailability in the availability calculation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine, the redundancy coordination logic, and the availability KPI calculation and reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry streams from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Includes operational status, fault events, performance metrics, and maintenance indicators. Required for real-time availability monitoring and anomaly detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional tasking interface. Inbound: operational tasks from Site Manager. Outbound: task status, availability constraints, and failover notifications. Required to coordinate availability assurance with operational scheduling.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety interface. Inbound: safety alerts and constraints from Safety Services. Outbound: availability status and failover decisions that may impact safety. Required to ensure failover logic is safety-aware.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Network connectivity for centralized monitoring, remote diagnostics, and predictive maintenance data exchange. Inbound: network status and connectivity metrics. Outbound: monitoring queries and analytics results.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of availability assurance. The centralized monitoring engine, redundancy coordination, and KPI calculation are system-level concerns that belong at this boundary. Minimal diffusion: only the Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces cross the boundary.

Vehicle could own local fault detection and autonomous failover for its own subsystems, but centralized availability monitoring and system-level redundancy coordination should not be contained within Vehicle. High diffusion risk: availability assurance would be fragmented across Vehicle and other subsystems.

Teleoperation could own local availability monitoring for remote operation continuity, but system-level availability assurance and redundancy coordination belong at the NGHE Solution level. High diffusion risk: availability logic would be scattered across Teleoperation and other subsystems.

Safety Services owns safety-driven shutdown decisions and safety constraints, but should not own the availability monitoring engine or redundancy coordination. High diffusion risk: mixing safety logic with availability logic could create conflicts and complicate verification.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of availability assurance because: (1) availability is a system-level KPI that requires visibility across all subsystems, (2) redundancy coordination and failover logic must be centralized to avoid conflicts and ensure consistency, (3) the Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces are already defined at the NGHE Solution boundary, and (4) this placement minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: Centralized monitoring creates a single point of failure if the monitoring engine itself fails. Mitigation: implement redundant monitoring instances and autonomous failover logic in each subsystem as a fallback.

Risk: Network Link failures could prevent centralized monitoring from detecting distributed failures. Mitigation: implement local fault detection in each subsystem and autonomous failover logic that does not depend on centralized coordination.

Risk: Availability assurance logic may become tightly coupled to specific subsystem implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), making it difficult to evolve subsystems independently. Mitigation: define clear, stable interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) and keep availability logic decoupled from subsystem internals.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: IM assignment is sound. Recommended refinements: (1) Define the internal architecture of the NGHE Solution module to separate the centralized monitoring engine, redundancy coordination logic, and KPI calculation into distinct sub-modules with clear interfaces. (2) Specify the latency and reliability requirements for the Telemetry and Network Link interfaces to ensure they can support real-time monitoring. (3) Define the failover decision logic and its interaction with Safety Services to ensure safety-aware redundancy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (centralized monitoring + redundancy) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP provides both observability (to detect and log failures) and fault tolerance (to mask transient faults and maintain operation). The coupling between FR and DP is clear and necessary. No over-constraint or under-specification detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If the centralized monitoring engine is not properly isolated from subsystem-specific logic, availability assurance could become tightly coupled to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services implementations. Mitigation: enforce strict interface boundaries and avoid direct dependencies on subsystem internals.

Diffusion Risk 2: If redundancy coordination logic is distributed across multiple modules, failover decisions could become inconsistent or conflicting. Mitigation: centralize all failover decision logic within the NGHE Solution module and expose it through well-defined interfaces.

Diffusion Risk 3: If the Network Link interface is unreliable, centralized monitoring could fail to detect distributed failures. Mitigation: implement autonomous local fault detection in each subsystem as a fallback.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: DP-D is well-contained within the NGHE Solution module. The module boundary is clear, and the external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined. No significant diffusion detected. The IM assignment supports the DP without introducing architectural fragmentation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible through operational telemetry collection and availability metric calculation. The NGHE Solution module can expose availability status through the Tasking interface (outbound) and log all downtime events for root-cause analysis. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of all subsystems to report operational status and fault events via Telemetry, (2) definition of the evaluation period and availability calculation methodology, (3) collection of historical failure data to validate predictive maintenance algorithms.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of FR-NGHE-004 to the NGHE Solution module is clear. The module owns the availability monitoring engine, redundancy coordination, and KPI calculation. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are allocated to specific subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) for local fault detection and recovery, with coordination at the NGHE Solution level.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine the DP to explicitly specify the failover decision logic and its interaction with Safety Services. Define the criteria for safe failover and the conditions under which failover is inhibited due to safety constraints.
Revision 2: Define the latency and reliability requirements for the Telemetry and Network Link interfaces to ensure they can support real-time monitoring and failover coordination.

Revision 3: Specify the internal architecture of the NGHE Solution module to separate the centralized monitoring engine, redundancy coordination logic, and KPI calculation into distinct sub-modules with clear interfaces.
Revision 4: Define the evaluation period, availability calculation methodology, and exclusions (planned maintenance, safety-driven shutdowns) to ensure consistent measurement and reporting.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into lower-level functional requirements to be implementable. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) require further decomposition to define the specific design parameters and implementation modules for fault detection, predictive maintenance, status visibility, and autonomous recovery. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify the architectural details and enable detailed design and implementation planning.
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified no blocking issues, but recommended revisions that are best addressed through lower-level decomposition. Specifically: (1) the failover decision logic and its interaction with Safety Services requires detailed design at the next level, (2) the latency and reliability requirements for Telemetry and Network Link interfaces should be derived from lower-level FRs, (3) the internal architecture of the NGHE Solution module should be defined through decomposition of the sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate and recover from transient faults without requiring manual intervention where safety permits.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services is available with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable real-time anomaly detection.
Assumption 2: Redundant subsystems can be provisioned and maintained within cost and weight constraints.
Assumption 3: The evaluation period for availability measurement is defined by the customer and is at least 30 days.
Assumption 4: Safety-driven unavailability (shutdowns initiated by Safety Services) is excluded from the availability calculation.
Assumption 5: Planned maintenance windows are excluded from the availability calculation.
Assumption 6: The Network Link interface is sufficiently reliable to support centralized monitoring; if not, autonomous local fault detection provides fallback.
open_risks: Risk 1: Centralized monitoring creates a single point of failure. Mitigation: implement redundant monitoring instances and autonomous failover logic in each subsystem.
Risk 2: Network Link failures could prevent centralized monitoring from detecting distributed failures. Mitigation: implement local fault detection in each subsystem.
Risk 3: Automatic failover could mask safety-critical failures. Mitigation: ensure failover logic is safety-aware and coordinated with Safety Services.
Risk 4: Predictive maintenance algorithms may have high false-positive rates. Mitigation: validate algorithms against historical failure data and tune thresholds iteratively.
Risk 5: Availability assurance logic may become tightly coupled to subsystem implementations. Mitigation: enforce strict interface boundaries and avoid direct dependencies on subsystem internals.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Historical failure data from similar NGHE systems to validate predictive maintenance algorithms and estimate achievable availability.
Evidence 2: Telemetry interface specifications (data types, latency, reliability) from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.
Evidence 3: Network Link reliability metrics and latency characteristics to assess feasibility of centralized monitoring.
Evidence 4: Safety Services interface specifications (SafetyAlert data types, decision criteria) to define safety-aware failover logic.
Evidence 5: Customer-defined availability target and evaluation period to finalize the availability calculation methodology.
Evidence 6: Cost and weight constraints for redundant subsystems to assess feasibility of DP-D.
